comment_text.sn21.2:1.2: Upatissa was his personal name, and he was the “son of Sāri”.
comment_text.sn21.2:3.6: Sāriputta became enlightened shortly after ordination (MN 74:14.4).

root_text.sn21.2:1.2: Tatra kho āyasmā sāriputto bhikkhū āmantesi:
root_text.sn21.2:1.4: “Āvuso”ti kho te bhikkhū āyasmato sāriputtassa paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn21.2:2.1: “Idha mayhaṁ, āvuso, rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn21.2:2.2: ‘atthi nu kho taṁ kiñci lokasmiṁ yassa me vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā’ti?
root_text.sn21.2:2.4: ‘natthi kho taṁ kiñci lokasmiṁ yassa me vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā uppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā’”ti.
root_text.sn21.2:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, āyasmā ānando āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn21.2:3.2: “satthupi kho te, āvuso sāriputta, vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā nuppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti?
root_text.sn21.2:3.3: “Satthupi kho me, āvuso, vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā nuppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā, api ca me evamassa:
root_text.sn21.2:3.4: ‘mahesakkho vata bho satthā antarahito mahiddhiko mahānubhāvo.
root_text.sn21.2:3.5: Sace hi bhagavā ciraṁ dīghamaddhānaṁ tiṭṭheyya tadassa bahujanahitāya bahujanasukhāya lokānukampāya atthāya hitāya sukhāya devamanussānan’ti.
root_text.sn21.2:3.6: Tathā hi panāyasmato sāriputtassa dīgharattaṁ ahaṅkāramamaṅkāramānānusayā susamūhatā.
root_text.sn21.2:3.7: Tasmā āyasmato sāriputtassa satthupi vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā nuppajjeyyuṁ sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā”ti.

translation_text.sn21.2:2.1: “Just now, reverends, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind:
translation_text.sn21.2:2.2: ‘Is there anything in the world whose decay and perishing would give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in me?’
translation_text.sn21.2:2.4: ‘There is nothing in the world whose decay and perishing would give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in me.’”
translation_text.sn21.2:3.1: When he said this, Venerable Ānanda said to him,
translation_text.sn21.2:3.2: “Even if the Teacher were to decay and perish? Wouldn’t that give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in you?”
translation_text.sn21.2:3.3: “Even if the Teacher were to decay and perish, that wouldn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in me. Still, I would think:
translation_text.sn21.2:3.4: ‘Oh lord, the illustrious Teacher, so mighty and powerful, has vanished!
translation_text.sn21.2:3.5: If the Buddha was to remain for a long time, that would be for the welfare and happiness of the people, out of sympathy for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of gods and humans.’”
translation_text.sn21.2:3.6: “That must be because Venerable Sāriputta has long ago totally eradicated I-making, mine-making, and the underlying tendency to conceit.
translation_text.sn21.2:3.7: So even if the Teacher were to decay and perish, it wouldn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress in him.”
